key_observations: Clear V-shaped recovery pattern: 4-week decline (13.7→6.3km) followed by strong rebound with volume spike to 19.3km in week 6, indicating planned deload and structured return to training
Heart rate elevation trend suggests increasing intensity—HR rose from 158→160→176 bpm over final 3 weeks while maintaining sub-6min/km pace, indicating improved aerobic power and readiness
Training frequency jumped from 1 run/week (May-early June) to 3 runs/week (late June onwards), with successful integration of football cross-training (Jun 27, HR 158) as additional stimulus
warnings: High acute-chronic ratio (1.18) in latest week with elevated HR (176 bpm) on a short 7.6km run suggests accumulated fatigue from rapid volume increase—monitor for overtraining
7-day gap between Jun 10 and Jun 22 breaks training continuity; limited data on recovery quality between deload and buildup phases
recommendations: Consolidate gains with 1-2 weeks at 15-17km volume at 160-165 bpm before attempting further increases; current 176 bpm spike indicates system stress
Maintain 3 run/week structure but ensure at least one true easy/recovery run ≤5km at 65-70% max HR to manage fatigue and support adaptation
Implement 1-week deload every 4 weeks (reduce to 10km total) to prevent cumulative fatigue—your physiology responded well to the Jun deload, so formalize this pattern

weeks_analysis.assessment: Baseline week; single long run at steady 6:14 pace. Good aerobic work but minimal frequency data.
29% volume drop suggests intentional step-back week. Maintained pace (6:11), preparing for deload.
Continued decline to lowest point (47% below baseline). Classic deload week—single short run.
Deload nadir at 6.3km. Pace improved to 5:47, indicating recovery quality. Minimal fatigue signal.
Return to 3 runs/week; +149% volume surge. HR 158 is controlled; training resumption successful despite 2-week gap.
Peak volume week. 3 runs including football (HR 158) and fast 5:59 pace runs. HR stable at 160—excellent adaptation window.
Unplanned drop to 7.6km after 19.3km peak; HR jumped to 176 on short 7.6km—fatigue signal. Suggests need for recovery or indicates intensity spike rather than decay.
